title: Lesson 2: New Identity in Jesus
description: Lesson 1 was about the Father running toward you. This lesson is about what He does when you arrive. He gives you a new identity in Jesus, an identity not built out of what you have done but received the moment you decide to come home.

blocks.text: Identity is one of the most pressing questions every person faces. Who am I? Most people answer with their skills, accomplishments, or what other people think about them. All of those answers can change tomorrow, but the gospel gives you one that never will. Your identity in Jesus is a gift, not earned by what you do but received by faith in what Jesus has already done.

Jesus told a story about a son who walked away from his father, wasted everything he had, and came back hoping to be treated like a servant. When the son came home, the father called for the best robe and put it on him. Identity in Jesus means God does not only forgive the road you walked. He covers you with the life of His Son and calls you His own.

blocks.alt: A father gently placing a robe over his returning son as the son's head lifts from shame toward his father

blocks.title: The Family Robe
blocks.text:
Picture the prodigal son standing in the road near his home, his clothes still stained from feeding pigs and his rehearsed speech ready. He is feeling guilty for his selfish decisions, and he knows he has no right to ask anything of his father.

But before the son can finish his speech, the father has already run to him and pulled him close. While the boy is still trembling in his father's arms, the father calls an order to the servants: "Bring quickly the best robe, and put it on him" (Luke 15:22).

The robe was not a reward for a well-planned apology. It was an announcement spoken over the son: you are home, you are mine, you are a son. The robe covered the decisions of the past without pretending there wasn't a wrong, and it declared that the father's grace was stronger than the son's guilt.
2.1 - Where do you still feel like you have to earn the robe before God will call you His child?
Put On Christ

The robe is a doorway into the truth of this lesson. Your deepest identity is not the worst thing you have done, the best thing you can do, or the way you feel about yourself on a given day. If you belong to Jesus, your identity is in Christ.
For as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ.

Paul says believers have put on Christ. When he speaks of being baptized into Christ, he means you have been so identified with Jesus that His life is now spoken over yours. To put on Christ is to be covered in Him. Jesus came as the perfect Son. He loved the Father with all His heart, soul, mind, and strength. He obeyed with clean motives, spoke with holy love, resisted every temptation, and lived the human life you could not live.

Then He went to the cross in your place. By faith, His perfect life is counted as yours, and your sin is counted to Him. The robe of righteousness is not you doing your best. It is Christ's own life covering you.
2.2 - Where are you tempted to build your identity out of your own effort instead of resting in Christ?
The Great Exchange

This is why Christians have often called the cross the Great Exchange. Your sin was placed on Jesus. His righteousness was given to you. Your old clothes were not cleaned up and handed back. You were clothed in someone else's perfect life.
For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.

That word righteousness means a right standing before God. It is not a mood, a personality type, or a spiritual confidence you have to manufacture. It is the settled place Jesus gives you by faith. His life covers you like the robe in the father's hands.

Walk through the exchange below slowly. Watch your sin land on Jesus, and His righteousness become yours. This is the truth beneath the robe: Christ takes what is yours so you can receive what is His.
Crucified With Christ
The exchange is not only legal, as if God only moved numbers in a ledger. It is deeply personal. The old you, the you defined by sin, shame, striving, and self-rule, was joined to Jesus at the cross.

I have been crucified with Christ. It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me. And the life I now live in the flesh I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me.

Paul does not say, "I improved myself with Christ." He says, I have been crucified with Christ. Christ took the old life under judgment. He took the punishment your sin deserved. Then He gave you a new life: His life in you, His righteousness over you, His relationship with the Father opened to you.

That is how a sinner can have a real relationship with a holy God. God does not ignore sin. He punishes sin in Christ. And God does not ask you to climb up to Him by your works. He brings you near in the Son, covered by a righteousness you received, not a status you achieved.
A New Creation
Paul does not just say you have been cleaned up. He says you have been made new. Your standing with God is not because of what you have done, but because of how you are covered.
Therefore, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come.

Trying to earn your standing with God through your own works will always make you either proud or afraid. Wearing Christ frees you from both. You can be honest about your sin because Jesus has taken it. You can be confident before God because Jesus has given you His perfect life. You can get up after failure because the robe is still His gift, not your wage.

Remembering the robe does not make sin small. It makes Jesus big. It gives you courage to confess because you are not protecting a fragile self-made identity. It gives you strength to obey because obedience is no longer a way to get loved. It is the family resemblance of someone already loved.
2.3 - What would it look like this week to live with quiet confidence that you stand before God wearing the robe of Christ's life?
This Week's Practice

Each morning this week, before the day gets loud, say this line out loud and sit with it for one minute: I stand before God wearing Christ. Then ask one honest question: where am I tempted to live today as if my own effort is still my identity?

Also this week, read Galatians 2:20 and Galatians 3:27 slowly. Let the two phrases stay with you: crucified with Christ and put on Christ. The old life went to the cross. The robe of Christ's life now covers you.
Lesson 2 Review

God does not leave you standing in the clothes of your old life. In Jesus, you have put on Christ: His perfect life covers you, your old self has been crucified with Him, and His life now lives in you. The robe of righteousness frees you from earning status with God and sends you into the week as a loved child. In the next lesson, you will meet the Spirit who makes this new life real in your daily walk.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson?
What is one step you are going to take this week?
What is a prayer request for this week?
